Stability is ionospheric physics, not dome evidence.
patches.replace:
for the duplicate-counting cross-reference.

The "quarter-wave doesn't apply at planetary scale" escape is closed in advance (preemptive). The strongest available defense against the pole-floor argument is to say f = c/(4H) is a parallel-plate simplification that breaks down at planetary scale, and the real Schumann mechanism is a transverse (TM) mode running around the cavity's horizontal dimension: fn = (c / 2πR) × √(n(n+1)) ≈ 10.59 Hz for n = 1 on a 6,371 km sphere, with the observed 7.83 Hz produced by ionospheric damping. This is correct globe physics — and it cannot rescue the dome for three compounding reasons. (i) The dome is already on record using quarter-wave. The model page (V50.6, “Schumann Cavity Height”) writes H = c / (4 × f_Schumann) = 299,792 / (4 × 7.83) = 9,572 km, and WIN-029 is the credit claim for that exact inversion. A retreat to transverse modes does not save WIN-038 in isolation; it requires the author to simultaneously withdraw WIN-029 and delete the 9,572 km firmament-height figure propagated throughout the model. You cannot have quarter-wave for the back-derivation you want and transverse-mode for the forward-derivation you need. (ii) Transverse mode imports a globe fingerprint. The spherical transverse formula reproduces 7.83 Hz only when the wavelength-setting length equals Earth's circumference, 2πREarth = 40,030 km. The dome's published disc diameter is 2 × 20,015 = 40,030 km — identical to four significant figures, because disc_radius = π × REarth is exactly the azimuthal-equidistant projection identity. Plugging dome disc diameter into the globe's transverse formula is the classic tautology flagged in Part 6 (STEELMAN-03: Tesla Disc Diameter Lock): a number built from globe geometry by construction cannot independently confirm dome geometry. (iii) The dome's non-uniform H(r) violates the transverse-mode boundary conditions. The fn ≈ 10.59 Hz formula assumes a closed, approximately uniform spherical shell; the dome's H(r) = 8,537 × exp(−r / 8,619) km varies by a factor of four across the disc and terminates at an open rim. Borrowing the sphere's mode formula without the sphere's geometry is precisely the silent formula-switch already documented in Section 2.1. There is no configuration in which the dome keeps its own geometry, uses a mode formula it has not committed to elsewhere, and arrives at 7.83 Hz — either the pole-floor holds (quarter-wave) or the globe is assumed (transverse).

Stability is ionospheric physics, not dome evidence.
patches.rationale: EXP-198: Inserts preemptive rebuttal closing the transverse-mode escape route. Analyst-authored paragraph addressing WIN-038.c3 defense_survives=3 (curmudgeon advocate rated the quarter-wave-doesn't-apply-at-planetary-scale defense as serious). Three compounding arguments: (i) dome commits to quarter-wave via WIN-029; (ii) transverse mode requires globe circumference = disc diameter (tautology); (iii) dome's non-uniform H(r) violates transverse-mode boundary conditions.

patches.find: at the Antarctic rim (r ≈ 17,000–20,015 km), the firmament ceiling H(r) ≈ 1,400–800 km. The n(r) values implied at these radii would produce gravitational-index coupling effects catastrophically inconsistent with GRACE satellite gravimetry.
patches.replace: at the Antarctic rim (r ≈ 15,500–20,015 km), the firmament ceiling H(r) ≈ 1,400–840 km.
patches.rationale: ISS-1297+ISS-1298: (1) Fix quantitative error: H(17,000) = 1,188 km not 1,400 km; use r=15,500 as the lower bound where H(15,500)=1,413 km so the 1,400 km figure is correct. (2) Remove vague 'gravitational-index coupling' closing sentence which asserts mechanism without quantification.

patches.find: a field-specific published dataset (IGRF, NASA station archives, ocean-tide gauges, telluric records, etc.) that was publicly available
patches.replace: GRACE/GRACE-FO Level-2 gravity products (JPL archive, continuous coverage since 2002) and published Bouguer-anomaly compilations (Pappa et al. 2019, JGR, DOI 10.1029/2019JB017997; Ferraccioli et al. 2011, Nature; Forte 2010) — all publicly available
patches.rationale: ISS-1299: Replace boilerplate generic source list with gravity-specific datasets appropriate for a gravity WIN.

patches.find: ~54,000–56,000 nT at the North Magnetic Pole and ~65,000–67,000 nT at the South Magnetic Pole per
patches.replace: ~54,000–56,000 nT at the North Magnetic Pole and ~55,000 nT at the geographic south pole per
patches.rationale: ISS-1300: Fix internal inconsistency. Paragraph 2 pins the dome's B(r) validation to ~55,000 nT at the geographic south pole; paragraph 3 was using the South Magnetic Pole framing (~65,000-67,000 nT). Update to geographic pole throughout for consistency.

patches.find: explicitly the INTERMAGNET ground-station network and the CHAOS-7 satellite-and-observatory composite that anchor mainstream geomagnetic models
patches.replace: the surface-observatory sources the dome's own home page highlights (INTERMAGNET ground stations and CHAOS-7, which the dome mischaracterizes as "paleomagnetic" but which is actually a present-day satellite-and-observatory composite) that anchor mainstream geomagnetic models
patches.rationale: ISS-1301: Weaken overclaim. 'Explicitly INTERMAGNET/CHAOS-7' attributes a specific source attribution to the dome that isn't verbatim in the dome text. Softer phrasing acknowledges these are the relevant sources without claiming the dome says so explicitly.

patches.replace: Dome adds no dome-derived equation — its FSF scaling formula is empirically calibrated per-station, not derived from disc-radius geometry;
patches.rationale: ISS-1303: Correct inaccuracy. Dome does publish delta_Z = baseline x coverage x FSF. More precise phrasing: 'no dome-derived equation' with explanation that FSF is empirically calibrated, matching WIN-010/025 phrasing.
